Which tools offer commercial licensing and under what plan tiers
Whether Midjourney allows API access or is still Discord/web-only

Adobe Firefly's Content Credentials and IP-safe training claims, and any independent verification of those claims

brief.research_plan: Prioritize official documentation and pricing pages for each tool for accuracy on cost and licensing. Look for independent technical evaluations and benchmark comparisons from AI art and photography communities (e.g., community posts on Reddit r/StableDiffusion, Midjourney Discord feedback threads, or creator YouTube channels that do side-by-side prompt tests). Avoid relying on the tools' own marketing pages for capability claims. For commercial licensing, go directly to terms of service pages. For real-world output quality, find creator forums or published prompt comparisons with visible outputs across identical or near-identical prompts. Note publication dates carefully since model versions update frequently.

brief.target_reader: Creators (musicians, hobbyists, filmmakers, content creators) with zero experience using AI music tools, who are curious but skeptical of hype and want a practical starting point rather than a sales pitch
brief.reader_outcome: The reader should be able to choose one AI music tool appropriate for their immediate need, generate their first piece of audio, and understand the basic constraints (quality, rights, cost) before committing to anything

Udio free tier: exact quota framing and commercial licensing status for free-tier users
Whether Suno and Udio currently allow stems or track separation for export, or only mixed audio

Whether any of these tools claim full copyright transfer to the user, and under what plan tier, per their official terms of service

brief.research_plan: Prioritize official documentation, pricing pages, and terms of service from each tool's own website for quota and rights claims. For capability descriptions (prompt types, genres, output formats), cross-reference official feature pages with recent first-hand user walkthroughs on YouTube or creator blogs published within the last 6 months, clearly distinguishing which claims come from official sources vs. user reports. Avoid restating credit or quota limits from third-party comparison sites without verifying against the official source, since these change frequently.

Tracks made through the Remix feature may lose commercial eligibility as jointly owned remixes are restricted to non-commercial use

Stem separation is accessed via the Edit menu

sources.title: Suno Advanced Stem Separation: What's New and Is It Worth It
claims_supported: Suno's June 11, 2026 stem update introduced three modes: Auto Split (splits into up to 12 stem categories), Split from Mix (isolates one selected element plus remainder), and Advanced Split (lets you choose from nearly 100 instruments to extract)
Advanced Split uses Suno's v5.5 generative model to regenerate each stem from scratch rather than carving out from the mix, reducing bleed and artifacts
Auto Split is available on Pro and Premier plans; Advanced Split is gated to Premier per independent reporting

sources.title: Beatoven.ai Official Website & Pricing Page

Third-party sources citing the vendor page as of Sept 2025 list a Creator plan starting at approximately $2.50–$7/month, metered by minutes of monthly downloads
Beatoven.ai claims a Fairly Trained certification, meaning models are trained on licensed/authorized music data only
Beatoven.ai grants users a non-exclusive, perpetual, royalty-free license on paid plans — Beatoven retains ownership of generated tracks
Paid plan tracks are licensed for commercial use including YouTube, podcasts, social media, and commercial video

unverifiable_claims: Exact current Beatoven.ai free tier quota (number of minutes or tracks) could not be confirmed from a live official pricing page crawl — the official page (beatoven.ai/pricing) returned minimal detail; third-party sources cite figures as old as Sept 2025 and show conflicting numbers ($2.50/mo vs $7/mo Creator plan). Writer must check beatoven.ai/pricing directly before publishing any specific figure.

Exact Beatoven.ai paid plan pricing tiers and minute allocations as of July 2026 — third-party aggregators show stale or conflicting data; no live official page content was retrievable to confirm current tier names, prices, or minute caps.

Loudly and Musicfy pricing and free-tier quotas — no official pricing page content was retrieved for either tool in this research session. Any claims about these tools should be dropped or independently verified directly from loudly.com and musicfy.lol before inclusion.

Whether Udio ever officially claimed 'full copyright transfer' to users — Udio's FAQ has been quoted by third parties as stating users 'own their output,' but without downloads currently enabled, this is described by observers as confined to the platform. The exact current Udio ToS language on copyright ownership was not retrieved from an official source and should be verified at udio.com/terms before being characterized.

Which specific Suno plan tier (Pro vs Premier) gates each stem mode (Auto Split vs Split from Mix vs Advanced Split) — the official Suno release note confirms the feature update but does not specify per-tier access in the retrieved content. Third-party sources suggest Auto Split is on Pro+, Advanced Split on Premier only, but this needs direct verification at suno.com/account or release notes.

Any claim that Suno offers 'full copyright transfer' to users — no such claim was found in official ToS. The ToS language is an assignment of commercial rights, not a copyright transfer; purely AI-generated outputs are not copyrightable under current U.S. Copyright Office guidance (Jan 2025 Part 2 Report). Writer should not use 'copyright transfer' language for either tool.
Any announced deprecations or major feature changes for Loudly, Musicfy, or Beatoven.ai — no official changelog or announcement was found for any of these three tools in this research session.

article.title: AI Music for Beginners: Make Your First Track Without the Confusion
alternative_titles: How to Start Making AI Music (Without Getting Burned by the Fine Print)
Your First AI Music Track: A No-Hype Beginner's Guide
article.dek: Pick a tool, type a prompt, and have actual audio in five minutes. Here's what to know before you start.
article.meta_description: A practical beginner's guide to AI music tools. Learn what Suno, Udio, and Beatoven.ai actually do, how free tiers work, and what the rights situation really looks like before you commit.
article.quick_answer: Suno is the easiest starting point for beginners: type a description, get a full song in seconds. According to Suno's help center (help.suno.com), the free plan includes 50 credits that replenish daily, enough for around 10 songs per day, with no credit card required. Free-tier output is personal use only, not commercial, per Suno's Terms of Service.
article.introduction:
AI music tools have gotten genuinely good, fast. You can type a sentence like "lo-fi hip hop beat, melancholy, rainy afternoon" and get a complete, listenable track in a matter of seconds. That's real, and it's worth getting excited about.

But the landscape is also a little messy right now: different tools have different limitations on downloads, commercial use, and audio quality, and the marketing copy tends to gloss over all of it. This guide skips the hype and gives you a clear path to your first generated track, plus the few things you actually need to know before you start.

sections.heading: What AI Music Tools Actually Do (and What They Don't)
sections.body_markdown:
These tools take a text prompt, sometimes a genre tag or mood selector, and generate audio using a model trained on large amounts of music. The results can sound surprisingly polished: full arrangements, vocals, structure, dynamics.

What they don't do, at least not yet on most tiers:

- Give you stems you can freely mix in your DAW (though Suno has added stem separation on paid plans, more on that below)
- Guarantee a unique output. The same prompt can produce similar results for different users
- Transfer copyright to you. Under current U.S. Copyright Office guidance, purely AI-generated outputs aren't copyrightable. (The methodology disclosure below notes this reflects USCO guidance as understood at time of writing; no single official ruling covers every scenario, and the legal landscape continues to evolve.) What tools like Suno offer instead is an assignment of commercial use rights on paid plans, which is a different and more limited thing

Think of these tools as a fast sketch pad. Great for demos, temp tracks, background audio, and inspiration. Trickier for anything where you need full ownership or stems to edit.

sections.heading: Three Tools Worth Starting With
sections.body_markdown:
Here's a quick take on three tools that are actually accessible to beginners right now, each good for a slightly different situation.

## Suno: Best for just getting started

Suno is the easiest on-ramp. You type a prompt, optionally add style tags, and it generates a complete song with lyrics and vocals in seconds. The interface is minimal enough that you don't need to read any documentation to get going.

Per Suno's help center (help.suno.com), the free plan includes 50 credits that replenish daily, which works out to roughly 10 songs per day. No credit card is required to start. According to Suno's Terms of Service, free-tier output is licensed for personal, non-commercial use only, and you must give attribution to Suno. If you want to use output commercially, that starts at the Pro plan. Check suno.com/pricing for the current monthly and annual rates, since pricing details may have changed.

On stem separation: per Suno's release notes (suno.com/release-notes/advanced-stems), three split modes have been released as distinct features: Auto Split (vocals/instruments), Split from Mix, and Advanced Split (individual instrument isolation). According to reporting from Dubspot Blog (citing Suno's feature rollout), Auto Split is available on Pro and Premier plans, while Advanced Split is gated to Premier. Confirm current tier gating at suno.com/pricing, as these details may have changed since that reporting.

One heads-up from the ToS: tracks made using the Remix feature may not be eligible for commercial use, even on paid plans, since Suno classifies remixes as joint works.

## Udio: High audio quality, but check export availability before relying on it

Udio produces audio that many users find particularly detailed and musical. A significant practical limitation: according to secondary reporting from Chartlex (published July 2026, citing Billboard and RouteNote), downloads were disabled following a settlement with Universal Music Group, and had not been re-enabled as of the time this guide was researched. Udio's own help center documentation does not address the download-disabled status directly, so check help.udio.com for current export availability before counting on Udio for a project you need to export.

For free-tier generation limits, Udio's official help documentation (help.udio.com) confirms free-tier users are capped at 3 u-130 generations per day. Chartlex (citing its own AI music comparison research published July 2026) additionally reports a monthly credit pool of 100 credits for free users, but that figure comes from that secondary source rather than Udio's official help documentation. Check help.udio.com directly for how daily and monthly limits interact and which figure is currently authoritative.

Regarding commercial rights on paid plans: Chartlex's secondary reporting describes Udio Pro as nominally granting commercial rights, but this was not confirmed directly against Udio's own Terms of Service or pricing page for this guide. Check udio.com/pricing and Udio's ToS directly if commercial rights matter for your use case.

## Beatoven.ai: Better for background and sync music

Beatoven.ai takes a different approach. Rather than lyrics-and-vocals songs, it focuses on instrumental background tracks for video, podcasts, and other content. You pick a mood, a genre, and a duration, and it generates something designed to sit under other audio without fighting for attention.

Beatoven.ai claims a "Fairly Trained" certification. Fairly Trained is a program that certifies AI companies for ethical training practices; check the public registry at fairlytrained.org directly to confirm current certification status and the organization behind it, rather than relying on company marketing materials. On paid plans, output is licensed for commercial use including YouTube and social media, and you get a perpetual, royalty-free license, though Beatoven retains ownership of the generated tracks. A free tier exists, but the specific quotas weren't reliably confirmed from official sources at the time of research. Check beatoven.ai/pricing directly for current details before making a plan around it.

tools.name: Suno
tools.description: Text-to-song generator with vocals, lyrics, and full arrangements. The most accessible starting point for total beginners.
strengths: Extremely fast to get your first result, and the free daily credit refresh means you can experiment without spending anything
limitations: Free-tier output is personal use only per Suno's ToS; commercial rights require a paid plan

tools.name: Udio
tools.description: High-quality AI song generation, currently limited to in-platform streaming only due to disabled downloads.
strengths: Audio quality is frequently praised for its musicality and detail
limitations: All downloads have been disabled since October 2025; you can't export audio for use anywhere else right now

tools.name: Beatoven.ai
tools.description: Instrumental background music generator aimed at content creators, with Fairly Trained certification and commercial licenses on paid plans.
strengths: Designed for sync and background use, with commercially licensed output on paid tiers
limitations: Instrumental only, not suitable if you want vocals or a full song feel; check pricing page for current free tier details

sections.heading: Your First Generation: A Simple Walkthrough
sections.body_markdown:
Using Suno as the example, since it has the lowest barrier to entry:

1. **Go to suno.com and create a free account.** No credit card needed.
2. **Click "Create" and find the prompt box.** You'll see options for a simple text prompt or a custom mode with style tags.
3. **Write a specific prompt.** Vague prompts get vague results. Instead of "happy music," try something like: *"upbeat acoustic folk song, fingerpicked guitar, warm vocals, about a road trip, feel-good summer vibe."* Genre, instrumentation, mood, and a loose subject or feeling all help.
4. **Hit generate and wait.** Suno will give you two variations by default. Listen to both before deciding one is bad.
5. **Don't love it? Iterate.** Change one element of your prompt. Add an instrument, swap the mood word, or try "no lyrics, instrumental only" if the vocals aren't working. Iteration is where the actual skill lives.
6. **Download if you want to keep it.** Suno's Terms of Service specify that free-tier output is for personal, non-commercial use only. If you want to download for personal use, verify your account's current download permissions at suno.com, since free-tier capabilities have changed over time. On paid plans, commercial use rights apply.

Your first result probably won't be perfect. That's fine. The goal of the first session is just to understand how the tool responds to your input, not to finish a track.

sections.heading: What to Watch Out For
sections.body_markdown:
A few things that catch beginners off guard:

**Rights are not the same across tools or tiers.** Suno's free tier is explicitly personal use only, per their ToS. Suno's Pro and Premier plans assign commercial rights to you, but this is an assignment of rights, not a copyright transfer. Current U.S. Copyright Office guidance holds that purely AI-generated works aren't copyrightable on their own. If you're making music for a client or putting it on streaming platforms, read the ToS for whatever plan you're on before you do anything.

**Litigation is ongoing.** As of mid-2026, Suno is still in active litigation with Sony Music and Universal Music Group, according to secondary reporting. A settlement between Warner Music Group and Suno has been reported by outlets including Billboard and RouteNote, as cited by Chartlex in its July 2026 comparison research. This doesn't necessarily affect your ability to use the tool today, but it's worth knowing the legal landscape isn't settled.

**Free tiers have real limits.** Per Suno's help center, the free plan includes 50 credits that replenish daily, good for roughly 10 songs per day. Udio's official help documentation confirms a daily generation cap for free-tier users (framed as 3 u-130 generations per day); Chartlex additionally reports a monthly credit pool of 100 credits, though that figure comes from secondary reporting rather than Udio's official documentation. Check help.udio.com for current details. And verify current export availability at Udio before relying on it for any project you need to take outside the platform.

**Stems and editing are still mostly paid or limited.** If your plan involves dropping AI-generated audio into a DAW and mixing individual elements, verify that your tool and plan tier actually support stem export before committing. Per Dubspot Blog's reporting on Suno's feature rollout, Auto Split is available on Pro and Premier plans and Advanced Split is gated to Premier. Confirm this against Suno's current pricing documentation (suno.com/pricing), as tier gating may have changed since that reporting.

**Quality is real, but not magic.** These tools can produce tracks that sound impressive on first listen and fall apart under scrutiny. Weird lyric choices, generic arrangements, and repetitive structures are common. Use AI output as a starting point, a sketch, or a reference, not always as a finished product.

key_takeaways: Suno is the easiest starting point: a free account lets you generate tracks without a payment method. Per Suno's help center, the free plan includes 50 credits that replenish daily (roughly 10 songs/day). Free-tier output is personal use only per Suno's Terms of Service.
Udio has impressive audio quality but all downloads are currently disabled (since October 2025), making it streaming-only for now. Not practical for a project you need to export.

Beatoven.ai is worth checking if you need instrumental background music with commercial licensing on paid plans. The company claims a Fairly Trained certification; check fairlytrained.org directly to verify current certification status rather than relying on marketing materials.
Free-tier output from these tools generally isn't licensed for commercial use. Commercial rights on Suno start at the paid Pro tier. Check the ToS for any tool before using output professionally.
Prompt specificity matters more than anything else. Genre, mood, instrumentation, and a loose subject give you far better results than a single adjective.
article.conclusion:
The fastest way to form an opinion about AI music tools is to just make something. Pick Suno, write a prompt more specific than you think you need to, and listen to what comes back. You'll learn more in 10 minutes of experimenting than from any amount of reading.

Just go in with clear eyes: these are creative tools with real constraints, not magic buttons. The rights situation matters if you're making anything commercial, the free tiers have genuine limits, and the outputs are starting points as much as finished products. But as starting points go, they're pretty remarkable ones.
article.methodology_disclosure: This guide was researched using official documentation, pricing pages, and Terms of Service retrieved directly from Suno (help.suno.com, suno.com/pricing, suno.com/terms-of-service, suno.com/release-notes), Udio (help.udio.com, udio.com/pricing), and Beatoven.ai (beatoven.ai/pricing). Capability descriptions and contextual observations about the current download status of Udio and the litigation landscape were cross-referenced against independent reporting from Chartlex (chartlex.com), published July 2026, and Dubspot Blog, which the editorial team treated as secondary sources rather than primary verification. Beatoven.ai free-tier quota specifics could not be confirmed from a live official page crawl and are therefore not stated as specific figures in this guide; readers are directed to beatoven.ai/pricing for current details. No hands-on testing of these tools was conducted by dailyblip for this guide. Claims about copyright and commercial rights reflect the ToS language and U.S. Copyright Office guidance as understood at time of writing; readers making real business decisions should review current ToS directly.

issues.original_text: downloads were disabled following a settlement with Universal Music Group
issues.issue: The article says downloads were disabled due to a UMG settlement, but the Chartlex source reports the settlement is with UMG and that a re-enabled licensed service is expected; the framing implies downloads are permanently disabled rather than temporarily suspended pending a new licensed platform launch, which is misleading about the nature of the situation.

issues.original_text: Chartlex (published July 2026, citing Billboard and RouteNote)
issues.issue: The article attributes the Udio download-disabled claim partly to Chartlex citing Billboard and RouteNote, but the Chartlex source states it was confirmed against Udio's own help center — misrepresenting the sourcing chain for a significant factual claim.

issues.original_text: Udio's official help documentation confirms free-tier users are capped at 3 u-130 generations per day
issues.issue: The article omits the 10 daily credits + 100 monthly credits structure reported by Chartlex and confirmed plausibly against Udio's pricing page, presenting an incomplete picture of Udio's free-tier limits by selectively citing only one figure from official docs.

issues.original_text: all downloads are currently disabled (since October 2025)
issues.issue: The article states downloads have been disabled since October 2025 as a settled fact, but the Chartlex source cites October 29, 2025 as the disable date per independent observation, not official Udio documentation — this should be caveated as secondary reporting rather than stated as verified fact.

issues.original_text: A settlement between Warner Music Group and Suno has been reported by outlets including Billboard and RouteNote
issues.issue: The article says WMG settled with Suno, but the Chartlex source reports it was Warner Music Group settling — while consistent, the article also says Suno is still in active litigation with Sony and UMG in the same breath, which is accurate but could confuse readers into thinking WMG is also still litigating; minor but the phrasing is muddled.

issues.original_text: per Suno's release notes (suno.com/release-notes/advanced-stems), three split modes have been released
issues.issue: The article says Auto Split splits into vocals/instruments (parenthetical), but the Dubspot Blog source reports Auto Split splits into up to 12 stem categories — the parenthetical description in the article is an understatement that mischaracterizes the feature.

The article correctly flags this as a company claim and directs readers to verify at fairlytrained.org, but the tool card in the same section describes it as a confirmed attribute without the same caveat, creating an inconsistency in how the claim is framed within the same section.

issues.original_text: Suno will give you two variations by default
issues.issue: The article states Suno generates two variations by default, but this specific default behavior is not confirmed by any cited primary source and may have changed — no Suno help center or pricing page citation supports this specific claim.

warnings: s2: The article says downloads were disabled due to a UMG settlement, but the Chartlex source reports the settlement is with UMG and that a re-enabled licensed service is expected; the framing implies downloads are permanently disabled rather than temporarily suspended pending a new licensed platform launch, which is misleading about the nature of the situation.

s2: The article attributes the Udio download-disabled claim partly to Chartlex citing Billboard and RouteNote, but the Chartlex source states it was confirmed against Udio's own help center — misrepresenting the sourcing chain for a significant factual claim.

images.prompt: A person sitting at a minimal wooden desk, seen from a relaxed three-quarter angle, wearing casual headphones and looking at a laptop screen that emits a warm amber glow. On the screen, barely readable, is a simple text input box — suggesting a prompt-based creative tool. The desk holds a small succulent, a plain ceramic mug, and a notebook with handwritten words. The room has soft late-afternoon light coming through a window with linen curtains. The overall palette is warm cream, amber, and dusty teal. The composition leaves generous open space in the upper-left quadrant for headline text placement. Mood: approachable, creative, self-directed — the beginning of something. No floating interfaces, no glowing brains, no robots.
images.alt_text: A person with headphones types a music prompt into a laptop at a warm, minimal desk setup

images.prompt: A close-up editorial flat-lay on a muted sage-green surface showing a smartphone displaying a minimal audio waveform, a hand-written sticky note that reads something abstract and illegible (just loose scrawl suggesting creative notes), a set of earbuds casually uncoiled beside the phone, and a small open notebook with rough genre/mood words sketched out as a loose list — all suggesting the process of going from a text idea to a generated song. The composition is slightly asymmetrical and editorial, with negative space in the upper right for text. Lighting is clean, soft, directional from the left. Color palette: sage green, off-white, warm charcoal. Mood: practical, first-steps creative workflow, not technical or corporate.
images.alt_text: Flat-lay of a smartphone with audio waveform, earbuds, and handwritten creative notes suggesting a first AI music generation workflow
